name: A Nation at Risk / modern standards movement
theoryOfAction: Raise expectations by making academic standards, assessment, and public accountability central to schooling.
inputVariablesChanged: State standards, graduation requirements, public reporting, assessment emphasis
evaluationDesigns: Historical synthesis; state trend evidence
outputsMeasured: NAEP, state tests, graduation requirements
normalizationIssues: Hard to isolate from demographic, fiscal, and accountability changes that followed.
oneLineFinding: Important agenda-setting reform, but not a discrete intervention with clean causal estimates.

name: Goals 2000 / standards-based reform
theoryOfAction: Encourage states to define rigorous goals and align curricula and assessments.
inputVariablesChanged: Standards adoption, planning grants, assessment alignment
evaluationDesigns: Historical/descriptive; state panel comparisons
outputsMeasured: State assessments, NAEP, graduation
normalizationIssues: State standards differed widely; implementation and assessment quality varied.
oneLineFinding: Helped institutionalize standards-based reform but evidence is mostly indirect.

name: State test-based accountability before NCLB
theoryOfAction: Use public test results and consequences to focus schools on achievement.
inputVariablesChanged: Test frequency, rating systems, sanctions/rewards, proficiency thresholds
evaluationDesigns: State panel, NAEP trend, difference-in-differences
outputsMeasured: NAEP math/reading, state proficiency, gaps
normalizationIssues: Researchers use NAEP to avoid state test inflation, but cross-state accountability intensity is hard to code.
oneLineFinding: Pre-NCLB accountability often raised measured math achievement more than reading, with risks of narrowing and gaming.

name: No Child Left Behind
theoryOfAction: Annual testing, subgroup reporting, and sanctions should pressure schools to improve proficiency for all groups.
inputVariablesChanged: Annual grades 3-8 testing, AYP targets, sanctions, public reporting, subgroup accountability
targetPopulation: All public schools receiving federal funds
evaluationDesigns: NAEP state panel; event study; state accountability comparisons
outputsMeasured: NAEP scale scores, state proficiency, subgroup gaps
normalizationIssues: Best comparisons use NAEP rather than state tests because state cut scores and test stakes changed.
oneLineFinding: Evidence points to some math gains, weaker reading effects, and substantial incentives to narrow curriculum or game thresholds.

name: Race to the Top
theoryOfAction: Competitive funding would induce states to adopt coherent reforms in standards, data, teacher evaluation, and turnaround.
inputVariablesChanged: Grant dollars, Common Core incentives, data systems, teacher evaluation, turnaround plans
evaluationDesigns: State comparisons; policy implementation surveys; achievement trend analysis
outputsMeasured: NAEP, state tests, policy adoption, teacher evaluation implementation
normalizationIssues: Winners differed from non-winners before grants; reforms were bundled and adoption was political.
oneLineFinding: Large policy adoption effects; achievement effects were not consistently strong or easy to attribute.

name: School Improvement Grants
theoryOfAction: Concentrated funds and prescribed turnaround models should change the lowest-performing schools quickly.
inputVariablesChanged: Turnaround, transformation, restart, closure models; leadership changes; staffing; extended time
evaluationDesigns: Regression discontinuity; implementation study
outputsMeasured: State test scores, graduation, school practices
normalizationIssues: Cutoff-based RD compares schools near eligibility thresholds, not all low-performing schools.
oneLineFinding: The national evaluation found weak average impacts despite large funding; implementation and model quality varied.

name: ESSA evidence tiers and evidence-based intervention requirements
theoryOfAction: Classify and encourage use of interventions backed by rigorous evidence.
inputVariablesChanged: Evidence tier rules, grant requirements, intervention review standards
evaluationDesigns: Evidence standards framework
outputsMeasured: Evidence tiers, study quality, implementation plans
normalizationIssues: Tiers are about evidence strength, not guaranteed local effect; external validity still matters.
oneLineFinding: Useful infrastructure for comparing studies, but it does not itself improve learning without good implementation.

name: Common Core State Standards and aligned assessments
theoryOfAction: Common, higher standards and aligned tests should make expectations clearer and improve college readiness.
inputVariablesChanged: ELA/math standards, test alignment, teacher guidance, curriculum shifts
evaluationDesigns: Implementation studies; teacher panels; state trend analysis; C-SAIL design
outputsMeasured: NAEP, state tests, teacher knowledge/practice, assessment alignment
normalizationIssues: Adoption timing overlaps with recession, NCLB waivers, RTT, assessment changes, and local curriculum decisions.
oneLineFinding: Implementation was uneven; evidence does not support the idea that standards alone generate large achievement gains.

name: Mississippi Literacy-Based Promotion Act / 'Mississippi miracle'
theoryOfAction: Pair early screening, teacher training, coaching, interventions, and third-grade promotion rules to improve early reading.
inputVariablesChanged: K-3 literacy instruction, phonics emphasis, reading coaches, intervention plans, retention/promotion threshold
evaluationDesigns: State trends; NAEP comparison; RD/quasi evidence on retention
outputsMeasured: Third-grade reading pass rates, NAEP grade 4 reading, state ELA, retention and exemptions
normalizationIssues: NAEP gains are state-level and bundled; retention changes who appears in grade 4, so researchers analyze both policy components and cohort composition.
oneLineFinding: Mississippi is one of the most cited recent literacy success cases, but the causal mechanism is a policy bundle rather than one isolated program.

name: Science of Reading state policy wave
theoryOfAction: Move teacher preparation and classroom practice toward explicit, systematic instruction in foundational reading skills plus language comprehension.
inputVariablesChanged: Teacher prep standards, licensure tests, curriculum bans/approvals, coaching, screeners
evaluationDesigns: Implementation studies; state trend comparisons; emerging causal work
outputsMeasured: Screeners, state reading scores, NAEP, curriculum adoption, teacher knowledge
normalizationIssues: Policies differ in what they mandate; teacher knowledge and classroom fidelity are not the same as law passage.
oneLineFinding: Highly influential and evidence-aligned in direction, but cross-state causal estimates are still emerging.

name: National Reading Panel synthesis
theoryOfAction: Identify components of reading instruction with experimental or quasi-experimental support.
inputVariablesChanged: Evidence review of phonemic awareness, phonics, fluency, vocabulary, comprehension
evaluationDesigns: Systematic evidence review
outputsMeasured: Effect sizes by reading domain
normalizationIssues: The review synthesized studies rather than evaluating a single program; later policies sometimes oversimplified the findings.
oneLineFinding: It became the foundation for much of the phonics/science-of-reading policy debate.

name: Reading First
theoryOfAction: Fund scientifically based reading instruction and assessment systems in high-poverty schools.
inputVariablesChanged: Reading coaches, PD, K-3 curricula, assessment, instructional time
evaluationDesigns: Regression discontinuity; randomized component; implementation study
outputsMeasured: Reading comprehension, decoding, instruction time, teacher practice
normalizationIssues: The evaluation tests the federal grant implementation, not the National Reading Panel findings themselves.
oneLineFinding: Improved some reading instruction measures but did not produce clear broad comprehension gains in the national impact study.

name: Success for All
theoryOfAction: Use a coherent literacy block, frequent assessment, grouping, tutoring, family support, and schoolwide facilitation.
inputVariablesChanged: 90-minute reading block, cooperative learning, assessments, tutoring, facilitator, family supports
targetPopulation: Pre-K to grade 8, especially high-poverty schools
evaluationDesigns: RCTs; WWC intervention reviews; meta-analysis
outputsMeasured: Reading achievement, phonics, comprehension, school implementation
normalizationIssues: Effects depend on fidelity and whole-school adoption; comparisons should account for program maturity.
oneLineFinding: One of the better-studied comprehensive literacy models, with positive evidence in multiple reviews.

name: Reading Recovery
theoryOfAction: Provide intensive one-to-one tutoring to struggling first-grade readers before failure compounds.
inputVariablesChanged: Daily 1:1 lessons, trained teachers, early identification, discontinuation rules
evaluationDesigns: RCTs; WWC review; longitudinal follow-up debates
outputsMeasured: Literacy achievement, writing, decoding, reading levels
normalizationIssues: Selection, attrition, cost, and fadeout are central; ITT and treatment-on-treated estimates can diverge.
oneLineFinding: Short-run reading gains are supported in some studies, but cost, scale, and longer-run durability remain contested.

name: Balanced literacy / workshop models
theoryOfAction: Build authentic reading/writing habits through leveled texts, mini-lessons, choice, and teacher conferences.
inputVariablesChanged: Leveled readers, independent reading, cueing, writing workshop, classroom libraries
evaluationDesigns: Implementation studies; curriculum comparisons; descriptive district trend evidence
outputsMeasured: Reading comprehension, decoding, motivation, state reading scores
normalizationIssues: The label covers diverse practice; some classrooms included phonics while others underemphasized decoding.
oneLineFinding: Popular and influential, but evidence concerns about weak systematic decoding instruction drove many recent curriculum changes.

name: Direct Instruction / Project Follow Through
theoryOfAction: Use explicit, scripted, sequenced instruction with high practice and immediate feedback.
inputVariablesChanged: Scripted lessons, mastery tests, teacher pacing, explicit modeling, corrective feedback
targetPopulation: Elementary students, historically including high-poverty sites
evaluationDesigns: Long-term follow-up; quasi-experimental and program evaluations
outputsMeasured: Standardized tests, dropout, college application
normalizationIssues: Follow Through comparisons are historically important but not designed like modern multisite RCTs.
oneLineFinding: A major counterexample to claims that highly explicit instruction is inherently low-level.

name: Core Knowledge / knowledge-rich curriculum
theoryOfAction: Reading comprehension and civic learning improve when students build systematic background knowledge across domains.
inputVariablesChanged: Sequenced content, literature/history/science knowledge, vocabulary, curriculum coherence
evaluationDesigns: Lottery/RCT in charter setting; quasi and implementation studies
outputsMeasured: Reading, math, science, state tests, vocabulary
normalizationIssues: Effects may reflect school model plus curriculum; generalizing outside participating schools requires care.
oneLineFinding: Recent causal work finds promising long-term reading gains, supporting a content-rich view of comprehension.

name: High-quality instructional materials initiatives
theoryOfAction: Better materials plus aligned professional learning can improve daily instruction at scale.
inputVariablesChanged: Curriculum adoption, EdReports-style signals, PL, district mandates/recommendations
evaluationDesigns: Teacher surveys; adoption studies; quasi evaluations
outputsMeasured: Material adoption, standards alignment, teacher use, achievement
normalizationIssues: Adoption is not usage; researchers measure fidelity, teacher adaptation, and alignment to avoid treating procurement as implementation.
oneLineFinding: Materials matter, but effects depend heavily on supports and classroom use.

name: High-dosage tutoring
theoryOfAction: Frequent individualized or small-group tutoring accelerates learning by adding feedback-rich instructional time.
inputVariablesChanged: Tutor-student ratio, session frequency, total hours, during-school scheduling, curriculum alignment
targetPopulation: Students below grade level; pandemic recovery; all grades with emphasis on math/reading
evaluationDesigns: RCTs; meta-analyses; implementation studies
outputsMeasured: Math and reading test scores, course passing, attendance
normalizationIssues: Normalize by cumulative hours, frequency, tutor type, group size, and whether tutoring occurs during the school day.
oneLineFinding: One of the strongest short-run academic interventions when frequent, sustained, curriculum-aligned, and attended.

name: Saga-style and tech-infused tutoring
theoryOfAction: Combine high-dosage human tutoring with software, diagnostics, and structured curricula to improve math.
inputVariablesChanged: Daily/scheduled tutoring, tutor training, diagnostic software, small groups
targetPopulation: Middle and high school math students
evaluationDesigns: RCTs; district pilots; cost studies
outputsMeasured: Math test scores, course grades, credits
normalizationIssues: Distinguish the tutoring relationship from software contribution; compare intended vs attended sessions.
oneLineFinding: Strong evidence for intensive tutoring models; the technology role is a support, not the whole treatment.

name: Project STAR / class-size reduction
theoryOfAction: Smaller K-3 classes increase teacher attention and student engagement, improving early learning and later outcomes.
inputVariablesChanged: Class size, teacher-student ratio, aide condition
evaluationDesigns: Randomized experiment; long-term administrative linkage
outputsMeasured: Early test scores, college attendance, earnings, savings, home ownership
normalizationIssues: Random assignment within schools is unusually strong; scaling requires enough effective teachers and classrooms.
oneLineFinding: Small early classes produced lasting benefits, but broad policy is expensive and sensitive to teacher labor supply.

name: Teacher coaching
theoryOfAction: Sustained, individualized coaching changes instruction more reliably than one-off workshops.
inputVariablesChanged: Coach visits, observation-feedback cycles, modeling, practice, video review, curriculum-aligned support
targetPopulation: Teachers, especially early literacy and math
evaluationDesigns: Meta-analysis of causal studies; RCTs
outputsMeasured: Instructional practice ratings, student achievement
normalizationIssues: Normalize by coaching cycles, coach expertise, content focus, and program scale; scaling effects are often smaller.
oneLineFinding: Coaching has stronger evidence than generic PD, especially at smaller scale and with clear instructional focus.

name: Traditional workshop professional development
theoryOfAction: Improve practice through training sessions, materials, and awareness of new standards or programs.
inputVariablesChanged: PD hours, workshops, summer institutes, training materials
evaluationDesigns: RCTs/quasi; reviews; implementation studies
outputsMeasured: Teacher knowledge, practice, student scores
normalizationIssues: Seat hours are a weak measure; compare content specificity, active practice, coaching, and curriculum alignment.
oneLineFinding: Often weak when isolated from coaching, materials, and accountability for classroom use.

name: Teach For America and alternate-route pipelines
theoryOfAction: Recruit academically strong candidates into hard-to-staff schools and support them with alternative preparation.
inputVariablesChanged: Selection criteria, summer training, school placement, support, service commitment
evaluationDesigns: RCTs/quasi evaluations; teacher value-added comparisons
outputsMeasured: Student math/reading gains, teacher retention, placement
normalizationIssues: Teacher assignment is not random in most settings; subject, grade, and retention matter.
oneLineFinding: Some evaluations show positive math effects, but reading effects and turnover tradeoffs vary by context.

name: National Board Certification and advanced credentials
theoryOfAction: Credentialing should identify and reward accomplished teachers, improving teacher quality and distribution.
inputVariablesChanged: Certification standards, incentives, teacher career ladders
evaluationDesigns: Observational/value-added; selection-adjusted comparisons
outputsMeasured: Teacher retention, value-added, student scores
normalizationIssues: Certified teachers are selected; researchers need prior performance and assignment controls.
oneLineFinding: Credentials can signal quality, but the policy question is whether incentives change who teaches where.

name: Teacher merit pay / POINT and similar incentives
theoryOfAction: Performance bonuses will motivate teachers to raise student achievement.
inputVariablesChanged: Bonus size, eligibility, test-score formula, individual/team incentives
evaluationDesigns: Randomized trials; quasi evaluations
outputsMeasured: Student test scores, teacher behavior, retention
normalizationIssues: Need account for baseline incentives, test stakes, collaboration vs competition, and whether teachers know how to improve.
oneLineFinding: Simple test-score bonuses alone have often produced null or modest effects.

name: DC IMPACT / teacher evaluation with consequences
theoryOfAction: Combine multiple teacher performance measures with incentives and dismissal risk to improve workforce quality.
inputVariablesChanged: Observation rubrics, value-added, ratings, bonuses, dismissals, professional development
evaluationDesigns: Difference-in-differences; regression discontinuity around rating thresholds; administrative data
outputsMeasured: Teacher performance, retention, student achievement
normalizationIssues: Compare teachers near rating cutoffs; distinguish incentive, selection, and support mechanisms.
oneLineFinding: More consequential evaluation can change teacher performance and composition, but sustainability and trust are central concerns.

name: Gates Measures of Effective Teaching Project
theoryOfAction: Use multiple measures to understand and predict effective teaching.
inputVariablesChanged: Classroom observations, student surveys, value-added, video analysis
evaluationDesigns: Research study with random assignment of classrooms in validation year
outputsMeasured: Measure reliability, predictive validity, student achievement
normalizationIssues: The project studied measurement validity; using measures in high-stakes systems is a separate policy intervention.
oneLineFinding: Influential for teacher evaluation design, but not itself a school improvement program.

name: Gates Intensive Partnerships for Effective Teaching
theoryOfAction: Better teacher effectiveness measures and talent systems should increase access to effective teaching.
inputVariablesChanged: Evaluation systems, staffing, compensation, tenure, PD, value-added, observations
evaluationDesigns: Longitudinal implementation and impact evaluation
outputsMeasured: Achievement growth, teacher effectiveness distribution, HR outcomes
normalizationIssues: Bundled reforms, local implementation, and changing district contexts complicate attribution.
oneLineFinding: The initiative did not deliver broad expected student achievement gains despite major investment and implementation effort.

name: Gates-funded small high schools / NYC Small Schools of Choice
theoryOfAction: Smaller, mission-focused schools improve personalization, engagement, and graduation outcomes.
inputVariablesChanged: School size, admissions choice, thematic focus, personalization, new school creation, closure of large schools
evaluationDesigns: Lottery-based evaluation using high school choice system
outputsMeasured: Graduation, credits, Regents exams, college enrollment
normalizationIssues: Lottery estimates apply to applicants to oversubscribed small schools; school closure and system redesign are part of context.
oneLineFinding: NYC small schools produced meaningful graduation gains, showing school design can matter when implementation and choice systems align.

name: Gates personalized learning / Next Generation Learning Challenges
theoryOfAction: Customize pace, path, place, and learner goals using data, flexible grouping, and technology.
inputVariablesChanged: Adaptive software, competency progression, student choice, flexible schedules, data dashboards
evaluationDesigns: Matched comparison; implementation study; site visits
outputsMeasured: Math and reading growth, student surveys, implementation measures
normalizationIssues: The treatment is a heterogeneous school model, not just software; compare active usage and teacher mediation.
oneLineFinding: Early RAND evidence was promising, but heterogeneity and later critiques caution against treating personalized learning as one proven model.

name: Gates Networks for School Improvement
theoryOfAction: Networked continuous improvement helps schools identify problems, test changes, and spread effective practices.
inputVariablesChanged: Intermediary support, CI cycles, shared aims, data use, student voice, cross-school learning
targetPopulation: Middle/high school transitions and postsecondary readiness
evaluationDesigns: RCT and quasi evaluation; implementation study
outputsMeasured: 9th-grade on-track, course failures, attendance, FAFSA, postsecondary enrollment
normalizationIssues: Impacts vary by network; researchers compare schools with stronger engagement and use targeted outcomes rather than broad test scores only.
oneLineFinding: The final evaluation found promising effects on some targeted outcomes, with engagement and CI fidelity tied to stronger results.

name: Charter schools overall
theoryOfAction: School autonomy plus family choice can create better school models and accountability through enrollment.
inputVariablesChanged: Autonomy, authorizer rules, school model, staffing, discipline, curriculum, CMO support
evaluationDesigns: Lottery studies; virtual matched controls; state panels
outputsMeasured: State tests, NAEP-like growth, graduation, college enrollment
normalizationIssues: Average charter effects hide major variation by state, authorizer, urbanicity, and school model; lottery samples are applicant populations.
oneLineFinding: Charter effects are highly heterogeneous: urban/no-excuses and some CMOs show strong gains, while many charters are neutral or weaker.

name: No-excuses charter networks and high-performing CMOs
theoryOfAction: High expectations, longer time, intensive tutoring, data use, and tight culture raise achievement in underserved communities.
inputVariablesChanged: Longer day/year, tutoring, behavior systems, teacher coaching, curriculum, data meetings
targetPopulation: Urban charter students, often lottery applicants
evaluationDesigns: Admissions lotteries; matched comparison; CMO evaluations
outputsMeasured: State tests, AP/SAT, college enrollment, behavior
normalizationIssues: Lottery applicants may not represent all students; attrition and peer/compliance mechanisms need scrutiny.
oneLineFinding: Some networks generate large test-score gains, but the debate is about replication, student experience, and which mechanisms matter.

name: Private school vouchers / DC and Louisiana examples
theoryOfAction: Giving families public funds for private schools increases match quality and competitive pressure.
inputVariablesChanged: Scholarship amount, eligibility, private school participation, admissions lotteries, accountability rules
evaluationDesigns: Lottery/RCT designs; administrative follow-up
outputsMeasured: Test scores, parent satisfaction, safety, graduation, college enrollment
normalizationIssues: Voucher effects depend on participating schools and accountability rules; compare offers (ITT) and actual attendance (TOT).
oneLineFinding: DC and Louisiana show why voucher evidence cannot be generalized: non-test outcomes may improve in some contexts while test scores can fall in others.

name: Magnet schools and controlled choice/desegregation plans
theoryOfAction: Use themed schools and assignment rules to improve access, integration, and specialized instruction.
inputVariablesChanged: Admissions rules, themes, transportation, racial/economic balance targets
evaluationDesigns: Lotteries; court-order changes; quasi studies
outputsMeasured: Achievement, integration, enrollment, graduation, parent demand
normalizationIssues: Goals include integration and access, not only test scores; applicant lotteries are not full-population effects.
oneLineFinding: Magnet effects depend on theme quality, selection, transportation, and whether integration is treated as an outcome or mechanism.

name: Community schools / NYC Community Schools
theoryOfAction: Integrated academics, health, counseling, family engagement, and community partnerships reduce barriers to learning.
inputVariablesChanged: On-site services, community school director, partnerships, family engagement, attendance supports
evaluationDesigns: Quasi-experimental impact study; implementation study
outputsMeasured: Attendance, chronic absenteeism, credits, achievement, graduation, school climate
normalizationIssues: Services, uptake, and neighborhood context vary; compare similar schools and track service dose.
oneLineFinding: Evidence is promising for attendance and some academic outcomes, but the model is complex and slower-moving than a single curriculum change.

name: Harlem Children's Zone / Promise Neighborhoods
theoryOfAction: Combine high-quality schools with cradle-to-career supports to change neighborhood trajectories.
inputVariablesChanged: Charter schools, parenting programs, health/social services, afterschool, college supports
evaluationDesigns: Quasi-experimental; school lottery; neighborhood comparisons
outputsMeasured: Achievement, college, family services, neighborhood indicators
normalizationIssues: Separate school effects from neighborhood services; treatment exposure varies by age and service take-up.
oneLineFinding: The strongest measured gains often come from the school component, while broader place-based effects are harder to isolate.

name: Social and emotional learning programs
theoryOfAction: Teach self-management, relationship, and decision-making skills to improve behavior, climate, and academics.
inputVariablesChanged: SEL lessons, teacher routines, schoolwide norms, adult SEL, climate strategies
evaluationDesigns: Meta-analyses; RCTs; implementation studies
outputsMeasured: SEL skills, behavior, mental health, achievement, climate
normalizationIssues: SEL outcomes rely on validated scales and can be politically redefined; implementation quality matters.
oneLineFinding: Meta-analytic evidence supports positive average effects, though program content and measurement vary widely.

name: PBIS / MTSS behavior systems
theoryOfAction: Improve behavior by explicitly teaching expectations and using tiered supports and data systems.
inputVariablesChanged: Behavior expectations, reinforcement systems, office discipline referrals, tiered intervention teams
targetPopulation: Whole schools; students needing behavior support
evaluationDesigns: Group RCTs; implementation studies; research database
outputsMeasured: Office referrals, suspensions, climate, achievement, fidelity
normalizationIssues: Discipline data are partly reporting artifacts; fidelity tools are needed to distinguish adoption from use.
oneLineFinding: Good evidence for behavior/climate improvements; academic effects are often indirect through instructional time.

name: Restorative practices
theoryOfAction: Replace exclusionary discipline with relationship repair and community accountability.
inputVariablesChanged: Circles, restorative conferences, staff training, coaching, discipline policy changes
evaluationDesigns: Cluster RCT; quasi studies; implementation evaluation
outputsMeasured: Suspensions, climate surveys, achievement, safety incidents
normalizationIssues: Suspension outcomes change when reporting and discipline policy change; academic effects may vary by grade level.
oneLineFinding: Can reduce suspensions and improve climate, but academic effects are not automatically positive and depend on execution.

name: Attendance and chronic absenteeism interventions
theoryOfAction: Improve learning by increasing presence and engagement through information, nudges, case management, and supports.
inputVariablesChanged: Parent messaging, attendance teams, transportation, case management, early warning systems
evaluationDesigns: RCTs of messaging; quasi district studies; early warning validation
outputsMeasured: Attendance, chronic absenteeism, course passing, achievement
normalizationIssues: Define chronic absenteeism consistently; compare pre-pandemic and post-pandemic baselines carefully.
oneLineFinding: Attendance is a crucial mediator after COVID, but interventions vary from low-cost nudges to intensive case management.

name: Head Start
theoryOfAction: Provide preschool, health, nutrition, and family services to improve school readiness for low-income children.
inputVariablesChanged: Preschool access, comprehensive services, family engagement, health screenings
evaluationDesigns: National randomized Head Start Impact Study; follow-ups
outputsMeasured: School readiness, cognition, parent practices, later school outcomes
normalizationIssues: Control children may access other care; fadeout on tests does not rule out later non-test effects.
oneLineFinding: The national RCT found early positive impacts with substantial fadeout on many early academic measures.

name: Perry Preschool / Abecedarian-style intensive early childhood
theoryOfAction: High-quality early education plus family engagement can produce long-run human capital and social benefits.
inputVariablesChanged: Low ratios, highly trained teachers, home visits, intensive early learning, health/nutrition supports
evaluationDesigns: Randomized experiments; long-term administrative/survey follow-up
outputsMeasured: Education, earnings, health, crime, welfare, intergenerational outcomes
normalizationIssues: Historic small samples and unusually intensive models may not generalize to scaled public pre-K.
oneLineFinding: Classic studies show large long-run benefits, but they are not evidence that every scaled pre-K program will replicate them.

name: Tennessee Voluntary Pre-K
theoryOfAction: State-funded pre-K should improve readiness for low-income 4-year-olds and later school success.
inputVariablesChanged: Pre-K access, curriculum, classroom quality, teacher credentials, state oversight
evaluationDesigns: Longitudinal RCT in oversubscribed sites; RDD component
outputsMeasured: School readiness, achievement, special education, discipline, retention, high school outcomes
normalizationIssues: RCT estimates apply to oversubscribed programs; quality and counterfactual care differ from other states.
oneLineFinding: A cautionary scaled pre-K case: short-run gains did not translate into broad long-run academic benefits in the evaluation.

name: Boston universal pre-K
theoryOfAction: High-quality public pre-K can improve long-run attainment and behavior even when test effects fade.
inputVariablesChanged: Public pre-K access, teacher quality, curriculum, centralized enrollment, classroom quality
evaluationDesigns: Admissions lottery; long-term administrative follow-up
outputsMeasured: SAT-taking, college enrollment, graduation, discipline, state test scores
normalizationIssues: Lottery applicants and Boston implementation may not generalize; long-term non-test outcomes are essential.
oneLineFinding: Shows that test-score fadeout can coexist with meaningful long-run gains in college and discipline outcomes.

name: Full-day kindergarten
theoryOfAction: More early instructional time improves readiness and early academic skills.
inputVariablesChanged: Length of kindergarten day, curriculum, teacher staffing, childcare replacement
evaluationDesigns: Quasi studies; district/state comparisons
outputsMeasured: Early literacy/math, attendance, later achievement
normalizationIssues: Need distinguish added instructional time from childcare and from selection into full-day programs.
oneLineFinding: Often improves short-run early skills, but durability varies with subsequent instruction quality.

name: Summer learning programs
theoryOfAction: Add structured academic and enrichment time during summer to reduce summer learning loss.
inputVariablesChanged: Program days, hours, attendance, teacher quality, curriculum, enrichment, transportation
targetPopulation: Elementary and middle school students, often low-income
evaluationDesigns: RCTs; implementation studies
outputsMeasured: Math/reading scores, attendance, SEL/enrichment participation
normalizationIssues: Attendance is pivotal; researchers compare invitation, enrollment, and high-attendance treatment-on-treated effects.
oneLineFinding: Benefits are clearest for students who attend enough days and receive well-planned instruction.

name: Extended learning time / longer day or year
theoryOfAction: More time can increase active learning, enrichment, tutoring, and teacher collaboration.
inputVariablesChanged: Minutes/day, days/year, block scheduling, teacher planning time, enrichment
evaluationDesigns: Causal synthesis; quasi studies; RCTs in specific models
outputsMeasured: Achievement, attendance, enrichment, teacher collaboration
normalizationIssues: Normalize by active learning time, not scheduled time; additional time can be low-value if poorly used.
oneLineFinding: Additional time has generally positive but context-sensitive effects, often small to moderate depending on dosage and use.

name: Four-day school week
theoryOfAction: Condense schedules for cost savings, teacher recruitment, or community preference, sometimes without reducing learning.
inputVariablesChanged: School days/week, daily minutes, total annual hours, attendance, teacher recruitment
evaluationDesigns: Difference-in-differences; state comparisons
outputsMeasured: Achievement, attendance, teacher retention, finances
normalizationIssues: Total instructional hours and district selection are central; not designed primarily as learning intervention.
oneLineFinding: Evidence is mixed and often negative when total learning time drops, making it a cautionary time-policy case.

name: One-to-one devices / laptop initiatives
theoryOfAction: Universal device access enables digital writing, research, collaboration, personalization, and equity of access.
inputVariablesChanged: Devices, broadband, LMS, teacher training, digital content, home access
evaluationDesigns: Meta-analysis; quasi studies; implementation research
outputsMeasured: ELA/writing/math/science scores, digital literacy, usage logs
normalizationIssues: Separate access from active academic use; device effects depend on teacher practice and content quality.
oneLineFinding: Meta-analytic evidence shows some positive average effects, but technology access alone is not an instructional model.

name: Adaptive and personalized learning platforms
theoryOfAction: Algorithms and dashboards tailor practice and feedback to each student's level.
inputVariablesChanged: Adaptive software minutes, mastery thresholds, item banks, teacher dashboard use, grouping
evaluationDesigns: RCTs; matched comparison; usage-log studies
outputsMeasured: Test scores, platform mastery, minutes, completion
normalizationIssues: Usage minutes are endogenous; compare teacher-mediated integration and curricular alignment.
oneLineFinding: Effects range from positive to null; platforms work best as part of coherent instruction rather than stand-alone screen time.

name: Generative AI tutoring and AI-school pilots
theoryOfAction: AI tutors or teacher copilots may provide scalable feedback, practice, differentiation, and planning support.
inputVariablesChanged: AI chat/tutoring, guardrails, teacher oversight, prompt design, feedback loops, usage logs
targetPopulation: Students and teachers in pilot schools
evaluationDesigns: Pilot studies; early RCTs mostly outside K-12; systematic reviews
outputsMeasured: Usage, engagement, homework, formative performance, teacher time, safety incidents
normalizationIssues: K-12 causal evidence is early; compare AI-mediated practice to human tutoring and to no-support screen time.
oneLineFinding: Treat AI as an emerging tool, not a proven replacement for teacher-led instruction or high-dosage tutoring.

name: Career Academies
theoryOfAction: Small learning communities that integrate academics, career themes, and employer links improve transitions to adulthood.
inputVariablesChanged: Career theme, academic/technical integration, employer partnerships, work-based learning, cohorts
evaluationDesigns: Random assignment longitudinal evaluation
outputsMeasured: Earnings, employment, postsecondary, credits, graduation
normalizationIssues: Random assignment applies to schools able to implement core features; adult labor outcomes matter more than test scores alone.
oneLineFinding: One of the strongest CTE-related causal studies, with notable long-run earnings effects especially for young men.

name: Modern CTE pathways and concentrator programs
theoryOfAction: Structured career pathways improve relevance, credentials, completion, and employment without sacrificing academics.
inputVariablesChanged: Course sequences, industry credentials, work-based learning, dual credit, employer partnerships
evaluationDesigns: Systematic reviews; quasi studies; administrative data
outputsMeasured: Graduation, credentials, attendance, college readiness, employment, earnings
normalizationIssues: Selection into CTE is a major issue; normalize by pathway quality, sequence completion, and labor-market alignment.
oneLineFinding: Evidence is increasingly positive for completion and college/career readiness, but quality varies by pathway.

name: Early College High Schools
theoryOfAction: Combine high school and college coursework so students earn credits and build college-going identity earlier.
inputVariablesChanged: Dual enrollment, small school design, college partnership, supports, accelerated credits
evaluationDesigns: Randomized/lms lottery evaluation; long-term follow-up
outputsMeasured: College enrollment, credits, degree attainment, high school graduation
normalizationIssues: Lottery/randomized access estimates are strong; compare credit quality and transferability.
oneLineFinding: One of the strongest causal postsecondary-readiness models, with positive impacts on college enrollment and degree attainment.

name: Dual enrollment / AP and advanced coursework expansion
theoryOfAction: Earlier access to college-level coursework raises expectations and reduces college credit/time barriers.
inputVariablesChanged: Course access, eligibility rules, tuition support, instructor qualifications, credit transfer, exams
targetPopulation: High school juniors/seniors and advanced students
evaluationDesigns: Quasi studies; administrative evaluations; descriptive trend
outputsMeasured: Credits earned, exam scores, college enrollment, persistence, degree completion
normalizationIssues: Selection is large; researchers use eligibility cutoffs, school fixed effects, and prior achievement controls.
oneLineFinding: Advanced access can help, but expansion must track pass rates, credit quality, and whether support keeps up with access.

name: Algebra for All / college-preparatory curriculum for all
theoryOfAction: Ending remedial tracks and requiring college-prep coursework increases rigor and access.
inputVariablesChanged: Required Algebra I, elimination of remedial tracks, course placement rules, curriculum requirements
evaluationDesigns: Interrupted time series; cohort comparisons
outputsMeasured: Course taking, grades, failures, test scores, college outcomes
normalizationIssues: Access metrics can improve while learning falls if supports are insufficient; compare course standards and failure rates.
oneLineFinding: The Chicago case reduced course-taking inequities but increased failures and did not clearly improve achievement or college outcomes.

name: AVID and college-readiness support models
theoryOfAction: Teach academic habits, tutoring, relational support, and college knowledge to students near the academic middle.
inputVariablesChanged: Elective course, tutoring, note-taking, college visits, teacher teams, cohort support
evaluationDesigns: Quasi/descriptive studies; implementation reports
outputsMeasured: Course taking, GPA, graduation, college enrollment
normalizationIssues: Selection into AVID and school implementation are hard to separate from program effects.
oneLineFinding: Widely used and plausible, but causal evidence is less clean than for early college lotteries.

name: School finance reforms / adequacy and equity funding
theoryOfAction: More and better-targeted resources can improve school quality, student achievement, and long-run outcomes.
inputVariablesChanged: Per-pupil spending, funding formula weights, capital and operating funds, staffing, salaries, class size
evaluationDesigns: Court-mandated reform event studies; difference-in-differences; administrative cohorts
outputsMeasured: Achievement, graduation, adult earnings, poverty, school resources
normalizationIssues: Use inflation-adjusted per-pupil spending, exposure years, district fixed effects, and pre-trend checks.
oneLineFinding: Modern causal finance research rejects the simple 'money does not matter' claim: sustained targeted dollars can matter.

name: ESSER and pandemic recovery initiatives
theoryOfAction: Federal relief dollars should support recovery through tutoring, summer learning, staffing, mental health, technology, and HVAC/safety.
inputVariablesChanged: Relief spending per pupil, tutoring, summer/enrichment, staffing, HVAC, mental health, edtech
targetPopulation: Districts and states after COVID disruptions
evaluationDesigns: District recovery scorecards; NAEP/state trends; spending/outcome analysis
outputsMeasured: NAEP, state tests, achievement recovery, absenteeism, spending categories
normalizationIssues: Spending categories are broad; local recovery depends on closure length, absenteeism, staffing, and baseline losses.
oneLineFinding: Relief funding appears to have aided recovery in many places, but which components drove gains varies by district.

name: Remote learning and virtual schooling during COVID
theoryOfAction: Maintain continuity when in-person schooling is disrupted or provide flexible virtual options.
inputVariablesChanged: Remote modality, synchronous time, devices, broadband, LMS, teacher adaptation, family supervision
targetPopulation: K-12 students during school closures or virtual programs
evaluationDesigns: Natural experiments; state/district closure comparisons; descriptive data
outputsMeasured: Learning loss/recovery, attendance, engagement, course completion
normalizationIssues: Remote schooling was not one intervention; closure length, home conditions, and prior district capacity are confounded.
oneLineFinding: Emergency remote learning was generally associated with large losses, especially when it replaced in-person schooling for long periods.

name: Family engagement and parent information interventions
theoryOfAction: Parents can support learning and attendance when schools provide timely, usable information and partnership structures.
inputVariablesChanged: Text messages, report cards, family conferences, home visits, parent training, two-way communication
evaluationDesigns: RCTs of messaging/info; quasi family engagement studies
outputsMeasured: Attendance, homework, course passing, test scores, parent behavior
normalizationIssues: Low-cost nudges and intensive home visiting are different treatments; language/cultural fit matters.
oneLineFinding: Information interventions can be cost-effective, but family engagement is not a substitute for strong instruction.

name: Competency-based / mastery-based learning
theoryOfAction: Students should progress after demonstrating mastery rather than after fixed seat time.
inputVariablesChanged: Mastery thresholds, reassessment, flexible pacing, standards-based grading, credit rules
evaluationDesigns: Implementation studies; quasi evaluations; emerging evidence
outputsMeasured: Course completion, grades, test scores, student agency, graduation
normalizationIssues: Grades and credits change meaning under mastery systems; researchers need common external outcomes.
oneLineFinding: Philosophically important but evidence remains heterogeneous and tied to local implementation.

name: Project-based learning and inquiry-oriented reform
theoryOfAction: Authentic problems and student inquiry build deeper understanding, transfer, collaboration, and motivation.
inputVariablesChanged: Projects, inquiry cycles, group work, performance tasks, teacher facilitation, interdisciplinary units
evaluationDesigns: RCTs in specific curricula; implementation studies; WWC reviews
outputsMeasured: Content tests, performance tasks, engagement, collaboration
normalizationIssues: Effects depend on scaffolding and assessment alignment; 'PBL' labels range from rigorous to superficial.
oneLineFinding: PBL is strongest when content-rich, well-scaffolded, and assessed on more than generic engagement.
